Alright, we've covered financial due diligence and commercial due diligence. Now we're rolling right into legal due diligence. And a reminder, you absolutely need a lawyer to do legal due diligence. You really shouldn't be representing yourself in legal matters unless you yourself are a lawyer. Even then, you should probably hire other lawyers to do your legal work for you. Now you might be wondering why we're covering legal due diligence at all. Well, as we discussed in the previous lessons, even though you're having an experienced professional due the bulk of your due diligence work for you and with you, you still want to be involved in the process. In fact, you have to be involved in the process. Even if you're not guiding the due diligence, you still want to be following along as the lawyer uncovers information, looks at contracts so that you can understand exactly what's going on behind the curtain. So the first thing a lawyer will want to see is the business title or the incorporation of the business. This will probably be an LLC, a limited liability company, an S-Corp, or maybe just a sole proprietorship. But there are quite a few different legal structures that it could be. What the lawyer is really looking at, or for, is to see the person who is selling you the business actually owns it, as well as what percentage of the business they actually own. They'll also want to verify what the legal structure is. The lawyer is going to spend some time going through and looking at the titles of all the assets within the business. That might be things like equipment, machinery, cars, IP, or maybe some other property, if that's something that's included in the sale. They'll also be making sure any websites, patents, trademarks, or other similar items are indeed owned by the business itself. Another part of the business they'll dive into are the employees. They'll look at things like contracts, benefits, and pensions. After all, once you take over the business, these people will become your employees. They'll check out the contracts, what the requirements are, the responsibilities of the new owner, etc. They'll also want to review any disputes or litigation. And these could be active or passive, present or past. You'll want to know if the business has ever been sued, or is currently being sued, or is at the risk of being sued. You do this legal due diligence to make sure that the legal contracts ensure that you, as the new owner, cannot be held liable. These type of things typically go through the insurance company for litigation or payment, but you still want to make sure that you're protected. And that's exactly what your lawyer is going to be doing. They're making sure you're protected from any claims or disputes or litigation in the past. They'll also review any intellectual property rights. This is going to be especially important for some companies such as engineering, pharmaceuticals, or anything that's online based. These types of businesses might have a lot of intellectual property attached to them. It's integral to the business, so your lawyer is going to make sure that all the paperwork is in order, and the business is properly formatted along with the paperwork, and that it's attached to the business entity so that you still have the intellectual property guarantee once you take ownership of the business. The lawyer will also look at any charges against the business or any assets that are being held as a guarantee. If the current business owner signed any personal guarantees for the business, that could actually help leverage the deal and the final negotiations. Your legal team will also want to review any contracts with suppliers and maybe help identify any areas where you might be able to negotiate the terms once the business changes hands. They'll review the shareholders agreement, articles of association, and memorandum of understanding. And these later documents essentially serve as a rulebook for the business. They'll tell you when the company has meetings, what's discussed at those meetings, who votes, etc. Provided that any of these businesses took those kind of notes. And one thing that your lawyer must look for is any potential drag along or suggested rights to any third party on the value of your business. A drag along right is a provision or clause in an agreement that it enables a majority shareholder to force a minority shareholder to join in the sale of the company. If the person you're dealing with owns 80% of the business, they can force the other 20% in the selling. And so it's an important aspect of legal due diligence that you and your lawyer will want to keep an eye out for. Your lawyer is also going to be looking at general insurance and product liability. You also want to review that the business has adequate permits, licenses, consents, and compliance to actually do business. If you're buying a dentist's office, law firm, or accounting firm, effectively any professional services firm, you'll want to verify that everyone in the business performing any work has current licenses, permits, et cetera, and that the proper paperwork is filed with the city, the state, the professional designation organizations, and that you're even allowed if you're a non-professional to acquire those firms. They'll also want to ensure that any business you acquire is compliant with the current environmental regulations and is meeting any expectations from entities such as the EPA that's applicable to the business. If you're buying a manufacturing facility or a construction company or a warehouse, you'll need to verify that all OSHA requirements are being met. Your lawyer should find out if the business has had any major issues with health and safety. That might not be a deal breaker, but you'll definitely need to know. They're also going to go through any potential insolvency events or any related party transactions. For example, maybe you're buying a business that works very closely with a particular supplier, but maybe the owner of that supplier is the brother of the business owner you're trying to buy the business from. In that situation, you want to make sure that all the contracts are legitimate and that the supplier is going to continue on after you take over the business and most importantly, at or better than the prices the business currently pays. Your lawyer is going to review the shareholder and director loans. In most places, it's legal for directors to loan or take loans from the business, but the lawyer is going to make sure that everything was done properly and that it meets the tax requirements for the IRS or whatever tax organization the business reports to. There are a lot of rules here dictating what the director of a company can or cannot do. So the lawyer is going to read through all the board minutes and resolutions, inspect any subsidiaries or branches to make sure the leases are all in order and everything is filed properly. What they find there then determines how you move to the next level. If there are multiple locations, then they're going to be gathering up and looking at copies of the statutory books or the share certificates. And they'll be making sure that anyone who says they're a shareholder is disclosed as opposed to one person with multiple share identities. That's all we're going to cover for legal due diligence. If this sounds overwhelming, please don't worry. But it is your responsibility to have a top-notch tax professional with you every step of the way, rolling up their sleeves and doing all the hard work and you being actively involved with it. We really want to be there absorbing all this knowledge. In our next lesson, we'll be moving on to financial due diligence. See you in the next lesson.
